key_insight: Survival models using time-indexed clinical inputs can accidentally learn how much follow-up was available from the record date, not the patient’s true event risk.
clinical_relevance: For cancer and other clinical prediction tasks, this can make model performance look better than it really is unless the study design allows enough follow-up after the newest inputs.
method: Defined and analyzed administrative-cutoff leakage as a distinct failure mode in survival prediction.
Used simulations to test how leakage affects fixed-horizon AUC and Harrell’s C-index under realistic follow-up patterns.
Validated the phenomenon in a real mammography cohort.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports simulation results plus a real-world mammography cohort showing inflated AUC and affected C-index when recent records have less potential follow-up.
watch_item: A model may appear strong in retrospective testing if input dates encode follow-up length; datasets should provide at least the full prediction horizon after the latest input date.
paper_title: Pitfalls of Administrative Censoring in Survival Models with Time-Indexed Inputs
